topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with its usual midday bustle, a comforting wall of background noise. Liam leaned back in his chair, swirling the lukewarm dregs of his latte, trying to focus on the quarterly reports spread across the table. Then, Sarah mentioned her new project—a high-level consultancy role in data analytics. Liam blinked, slowly. He’d known Sarah for years; she was fiercely passionate about her work, always animated when discussing her career trajectory. But something felt… off. He’d seen the LinkedIn profile, a generic description, nothing concrete. When Sarah spoke about client meetings, her eyes darted away, and she stammered when pressed for specifics about the actual firm. A cold knot tightened in Liam’s stomach. He felt a sudden, strange urge to check her phone, a feeling that made his hands tremble slightly on the table.

[story 2]

"So, you're really going into astrophysics?" Mark asked, his voice laced with genuine curiosity. Maya offered a tight, practiced smile that didn't quite reach her eyes. She fiddled with the strap of her messenger bag, her fingers tracing the worn leather repeatedly. Mark pressed on, asking about the specifics of her research, the labs she’d been visiting. Maya’s composure began to fray at the edges. She laughed, a sound that felt brittle and forced, and quickly changed the subject to something trivial, like the weather. Internally, Maya was racing, constructing a defense mechanism against the sudden exposure of her fabrication. Every time Mark looked at her with expectant warmth, she felt a rising pressure behind her ribs, a desperate need to just disappear into the patterned wallpaper of the cafe.

[story 3]

I watched Alex pace the length of the living room floor. He kept stopping, running a hand through his already messy hair, his gaze fixed on the framed photo on the mantelpiece. He’d been telling me, quite earnestly, that he was transitioning into a senior role at the tech startup downtown. I had been trying to be supportive, nodding along, but the details he offered kept dissolving into vague generalizations. When I asked him to show me his new company website, he’d become suddenly very animated, launching into a detailed description of a product line that sounded entirely fabricated. A heavy silence settled between us, thick and suffocating. I just stared at him, my mouth slightly agape, waiting for him to bridge the gap, but he just looked away, his shoulders slumping in a way that spoke volumes about his internal struggle.
